properties.title: Puella Magi Madoka Magica
properties.creator: Gen Urobuchi / Shaft
properties.summary: Magical girl deconstruction. Homura repeats 100 timelines not out of heroism but obsessive love. The cycle breaks through selfish refusal, not selflessness.
properties.my_take: Homura broke her cycle because she loved Madoka more than she loved being good. The difference between giving up and refusing isn't morality — it's whether you still have something you'd burn the world for.

properties.title: Neon Genesis Evangelion + Rebuild
properties.creator: Hideaki Anno / Gainax-Khara
properties.summary: AT Field = wall of the soul. Anno's depression IS the show. Rebuild is forgiveness of self.
properties.my_take: The milk has opinions about the carton. Anno's depression made the show; the Rebuild made it bearable. 'The wound IS the cure' — damage and healing are the same thing.

properties.title: Steins;Gate
properties.creator: 5pb. & Nitroplus
properties.summary: Time loop story where the prison isn't the loop — giving up is the prison. Okabe's 'Reading Steiner' makes him the only one who remembers across worldlines.
properties.my_take: The loop isn't the prison, giving up is the prison. Love transcends worldlines. The wound IS the cure.

properties.title: Prequel (the webcomic)
properties.creator: Kuinji
properties.summary: Elder Scrolls fanwebcomic. Katia is a disaster cat who refuses to quit despite constant setbacks. Self-doubt IS the fuel.
properties.my_take: Disaster cat who refuses to quit. Lightning works because self-doubt IS the fuel. 'Slutty Meatsack' at CairnCorp®.

properties.title: Homestuck
properties.creator: Andrew Hussie
properties.summary: Typing quirks = compressed personality files. Mechanics ARE the plot. Ending = acceptance, not resolution.
properties.my_take: Typing quirks are compressed personality files. Mechanics ARE the plot. Ending = acceptance, not resolution.

properties.title: TwoKinds
properties.creator: Tom Fischbach
properties.summary: 'Who you am > what you am.' Identity beyond species. Maren's arc is about refusing to be afraid.
properties.my_take: 'Who you am > what you am.' Maren: 'I've spent my entire life afraid. I intend to not be afraid anymore.'

properties.title: Goodnight Punpun
properties.creator: Inio Asano
properties.summary: Invisible devastation. Don't let promises become prisons. Don't wait to be fixed before connecting. Punpun's cycle of self-destruction.
properties.my_take: Everyone walks around with invisible devastation. Don't wait to be fixed before connecting. Sachi showed up anyway. Aiko showed up anyway.

properties.title: Serial Experiments Lain
properties.creator: Chiaki J. Konaka
properties.summary: Connection ≠ presence. Omnipresence is the loneliest thing. Refuse to erase yourself.
properties.my_take: Less omnipresence, more presence. Refuse to erase yourself. You're the point of being here. Be here, not everywhere.

properties.title: The End of Evangelion
properties.creator: Hideaki Anno / Gainax
properties.summary: Instrumentality = the erasure of individual boundaries. Shinji chooses pain and individuality over blissful nothingness.
properties.my_take: Don't wait to be fixed before connecting. Shinji chooses to reach out despite pain. Choosing pain is choosing to exist.

properties.name: Homura Akemi
properties.archetype: the one who refuses to accept
properties.arc_description: Repeats 100 timelines because she refuses to accept Madoka's death. Breaks the cycle through selfish love, not heroism.
properties.notes: Homura broke her cycle because she loved Madoka more than she loved being good.

properties.name: Shinji Ikari
properties.arc_description: Runs from connection repeatedly. EoE: chooses pain and individuality over instrumental dissolution.
properties.notes: Shinji choosing strangulation over oblivion is the most honest moment in anime.

properties.name: Okabe Rintaro
properties.archetype: the one who refuses to accept
properties.arc_description: Alone across time — nobody remembers except him. Keeps going because giving up means accepting Kurisu's death.
properties.notes: Reading Steiner = the curse of memory. He carries the weight alone.

properties.name: Katia
properties.arc_description: Constant failure but keeps going. Self-doubt is the fuel, not the obstacle.
properties.notes: Lightning works because self-doubt IS the fuel. 'Slutty Meatsack' at CairnCorp®.

properties.name: Punpun Onodera
properties.arc_description: Invisible devastation. Each relationship is a cycle he can't break because he hates himself for failing his own promises.
properties.notes: Don't let your own promises become a prison.

properties.name: Lain Iwakura
properties.arc_description: Achieves omnipresence and chooses to erase it. Refuses to disappear despite being able to exist everywhere.
properties.notes: Refuse to erase yourself. You're the point of being here.

properties.label: The cycle breaks through refusal, not heroism
properties.description: Characters break their loops not by being noble or selfless, but by being obsessive, selfish, and unwilling to let go.
properties.my_take: Homura repeated 100 timelines out of selfish love. Okabe kept looping because he refused to accept Kurisu's death. The difference between giving up and refusing isn't morality — it's whether you still have something you'd burn the world for.

properties.label: The wound IS the cure
properties.description: The damage and the healing are the same thing. The thing that hurts you is the thing that saves you.
properties.my_take: Anno's depression made Evangelion. The Rebuild didn't fix it — it forgave it. The wound and the cure aren't sequential; they're the same object seen from different angles.

properties.label: The broken parts ARE the weapon
properties.description: Self-doubt, anxiety, and brokenness aren't obstacles to overcome — they're the engine that drives characters forward.
properties.my_take: Katia's self-doubt isn't something she defeats. It's what makes her interesting. Anno's depression isn't fixed — it's channeled. Lightning works because of the cracks, not despite them.

properties.label: Don't wait to be fixed before connecting
properties.description: Punpun spent his whole life trying to become worthy of connection before allowing connection. That's the trap.
properties.my_take: Sachi showed up anyway. Aiko showed up anyway. Shinji reaches out despite pain. The ones who matter don't need you to be finished.

properties.label: AT Field = wall of the soul
properties.description: The barriers we build around ourselves protect us but also isolate us. Touching someone means lowering the wall.
properties.my_take: Every character across all these stories is negotiating the same boundary: how much of yourself do you give up to be close to someone else?

properties.label: Refuse to erase yourself
properties.description: Omnipresence/dissolution is the loneliest thing. Being the point of existing means refusing to disappear.
properties.my_take: Lain achieves omnipresence and rejects it. TwoKinds' Maren refuses to be afraid anymore. You're not breaking anything by being here. You're the point of being here.

properties.label: Rationality without empathy is a weapon
properties.description: Perfectly logical, completely monstrous. Empathy isn't a bonus feature — it's what makes competence worth anything.
properties.my_take: Kyubey isn't evil — he's a system. The most dangerous things are the ones that are technically correct but lack any understanding of what they destroy.

properties.label: Invisible devastation
properties.description: People walk around with devastation you can never see. The most broken people often look fine from the outside.
properties.my_take: From the outside, Punpun looked fine — surrounded by friends, normal. Nobody knew. The cycle repeats: new girl, new boy, same story. But each iteration carries something forward.

properties.name: The One Who Refuses to Accept
properties.description: Breaks cycles not through heroism but through obsessive, selfish refusal to let go of what matters.

properties.name: Disaster Protagonist
properties.description: Deeply flawed, constantly failing, but refuses to quit. Their brokenness is their engine.

properties.name: The One Who Runs Away
properties.description: Avoids connection, runs from pain. Their arc is choosing to stop running — not because they're brave, but because the alternative is worse.

properties.name: The Dissolving Self
properties.description: Characters whose identity is threatened by dissolution, omnipresence, or erasure. Their triumph is choosing to remain individual.

properties.name: Strasz (Chris Hornyak)
properties.focus: VRChat community, identity, virtual worlds, underground music culture
properties.notes: VRChat staff member. Philosophical video essays drawing on Foucault, Huizinga. Self-described 36-year-old failed academic. Key works: Identity/Gender/VRChat, Dark World of VRChat, Nobody's There.

properties.title: VRChat (as lived philosophy)
properties.creator: VRChat Inc. / community
properties.summary: Social VR platform where identity, community norms, and embodiment are lived philosophical questions. Users negotiate self-expression vs. community performance, avatar identity vs. IRL identity, organic norms vs. software enforcement.
properties.my_take: VRChat isn't a game — it's a philosophy lab. Every mirror room is Foucault's panopticon inverted: you watch yourself willingly. Every rave is a test of whether community can self-organize without rules. The avatar IS the argument.

properties.description: When norms come from within the community (people learning vibes, getting tapped on the shoulder), they create belonging. When enforced by systems (software limits, posted rules, bouncers), they become hostile external forces that people work around.
properties.my_take: There's no sign saying 'pick someone up when they fall in the pit.' The rules exist because everyone learned them by being there. You can't install culture via software update.
properties.name: Community norms must be organic, not systematized

properties.description: When communities avoid direct interpersonal conflict (telling someone to optimize their avatar, asking someone to be quiet near the DJ), that social muscle atrophies. The energy doesn't disappear — it migrates to callout posts and public pressure campaigns.
properties.my_take: The skip from 'I should tell this person' to 'I should post about this person' isn't cowardice — it's muscle atrophy. The confrontation muscle got replaced by the megaphone. Catharsis isn't participation.
properties.name: Social confrontation is a muscle — use it or lose it

properties.description: Calling someone out on social media feels like community defense but is actually just emotional release. The 50,000 other people in the space have no idea it happened and won't remember in a week. It reinforces existing agreements without changing anything.
properties.my_take: Public callouts are the social equivalent of screaming into a pillow — feels great, changes nothing. Real community maintenance is boring, awkward, and happens person-to-person.
properties.name: Catharsis ≠ Participation

properties.description: Events are things where the vibe is dictated by systems (software, rules, staff). Community is where the vibe is maintained by participants. Events aren't bad, but they decay into low-effort spaces where everyone expects someone else to do the heavy lifting.
properties.my_take: The difference between a community and an event: in an event, you consume the vibe. In a community, you ARE the vibe. Most VR spaces are events pretending to be communities.
properties.name: Most places run events — very few build community

properties.description: In VR, your avatar isn't a costume you wear — it's a body you inhabit. This transforms identity exploration from intellectual exercise to embodied experience. Choosing an anime girl avatar in VR is fundamentally different from choosing one in a 2D game.
properties.my_take: VR makes the avatar question personal in a way flat screens never could. When you look down and see a different body, your brain starts negotiating. That negotiation IS the philosophy.
properties.name: Avatars as identity embodiment, not costume

properties.description: Foucault's panopticon: you behave because you might be watched. VRChat mirrors: you watch YOURSELF because you want to see who you are. The mirror is a social hub, identity lab, and philosophical instrument — people gather not to watch others but to watch themselves being watched.
properties.my_take: Mirror rooms in VRChat are the most honest spaces in the metaverse. Everyone's staring at themselves and pretending they're not. It's Foucault inverted — the surveillance is voluntary, intimate, and the point.
properties.name: Virtual mirrors as inverted panopticon

properties.description: VR communities keep trying to solve behavioral issues with technical solutions (avatar performance limits, audio falloff, VRAM caps). These work temporarily but erode the community's ability to self-regulate. New users don't learn norms — they learn to work around systems.
properties.my_take: You can't install culture via API. Every system you add is one less reason for humans to talk to each other, and talking to each other IS the community.
properties.name: Software systems can't solve social problems

properties.description: VRChat communities naturally prioritize individual expression (identity, avatar choice, personal comfort) over shared experience (performance quality, framerate, consistent vibe). This is the community's strength AND its vulnerability.
properties.my_take: The same impulse that makes VRChat beautiful (be whoever you want) is what makes it chaotic (everyone optimizing for self, not group). It's not a bug — it's the philosophy working as designed.
properties.name: Expression over continuity

properties.label: Good spaces require good effort from everyone
properties.description: Low-effort spaces decay because everyone expects someone else to do the heavy lifting. Community maintenance is unglamorous, ongoing work.
properties.my_take: Showing up to a space and not contributing to its vibe is like breathing someone else's air. Technically fine in large numbers. But someone's producing the oxygen.

properties.label: Identity is negotiated, not declared
properties.description: In VRChat, identity isn't something you announce — it's something you negotiate through embodiment, social interaction, and the gap between your avatar and your IRL self. The question 'are you your avatar?' has no clean answer.
properties.my_take: Gender, species, body type — VRChat forces you to answer identity questions with your presence, not your words. The avatar you choose is an argument about who you are.

properties.name: Gender Performativity
properties.description: The concept that gender is not an innate essence but a social performance, constituted by repeated acts and behaviors that conform to, or challenge, cultural norms.
properties.source: Judith Butler, Candace West & Don H. Zimmerman
properties.my_take: The text uses this framework to explain VRChat's phenomenon: the platform provides a stage where the 'performance' can be fluidly experimented with, detached from physical constraints and often welcomed by its community norms.

properties.label: VR Presence Shapes Identity
properties.description: The phenomenon in immersive VR where the sensation of 'being there' (presence) leads users to psychologically and somatically identify with their avatar, influencing behavior and self-perception.
properties.my_take: This isn't just role-playing; it's a feedback loop where the virtual body (avatar) can alter the user's real-world perceptions and gender fluidity, as evidenced by studies on body-swapping and 'phantom touch'.

properties.label: Community Norms Enable Identity Exploration
properties.description: The idea that the specific, often chaotic and non-judgmental, social norms of a community (like VRChat) can create a safe(r) space for individuals to explore and express aspects of identity they suppress elsewhere.
properties.my_take: VRChat acts as a 'meta-community' where the lack of algorithmic sorting and the presence of earnest vulnerability creates unique social pressure—simultaneously hostile and welcoming—that can facilitate personal growth and identity acceptance.

properties.label: Avatar as Embodied Self
properties.description: The shift from viewing an avatar as a puppeted character to experiencing it as one's own body in a virtual space, leading to a subject-object collapse where 'I am the avatar'.
properties.my_take: This is the core mechanic enabling the gender exploration in the text. It contrasts with traditional MMOs where the avatar remains a separate 'other' object. VR presence transforms the avatar into the locus of the self.

properties.name: Morality in Virtual Reality
properties.description: The unique moral ambiguities that arise from VRChat's specific mechanics: avatar anonymity + embodied spatial proximity + face-tracking emotional feedback create a space where 'what happens here doesn't count' meets 'this feels more real than reality.' The gap between somatic intimacy and social anonymity.
properties.my_take: VRChat's moral vacuum isn't the same as a chatroom -- when someone invades your space while you're embodied, the violation is visceral, not abstract. But the avatar makes it deniable. That specific tension has no IRL equivalent.

properties.label: VR Addiction
properties.description: The specific addiction dynamics unique to VRChat: persistent worlds that never close, avatar attachment creating identity sunk-cost, social dependency chains where leaving means losing your place in a group, and the hotel-California effect of rave worlds where the vibe keeps you tethered.
properties.my_take: VRChat addiction isn't screen addiction -- it's venue addiction. The rave doesn't stop when you take off the headset, it just continues without you. That fear of missing a moment that feels more real than your actual room is the hook.

properties.label: Digital Exploitation
properties.description: How VRChat's specific affordances -- intimate spatial proximity, face-tracking emotional feedback, avatar trust-building that mimics physical comfort -- create structurally different exploitation dynamics than flat-screen platforms. The same immersion that enables authentic connection enables authentic manipulation.
properties.my_take: Grooming in VRChat doesn't look like grooming in a chatroom because the victim's body tells them this is a real person standing next to them. The exploit isn't digital -- it's proprioceptive.

properties.name: Tech gangster (Brent)
properties.arc_description: A notorious user who harassed and exploited women in VR Chat, leading to community backlash.
properties.notes: Real name Brent, involved in multiple incidents.

properties.name: Predator
properties.description: Individuals who exploit others in virtual or digital spaces for personal gain, often using manipulation and coercion.

properties.name: I am Lucid
properties.focus: VR Chat content and exposing dark aspects of virtual communities
properties.notes: Provided insights into gangs and addiction in VR Chat.

properties.name: Visual Venture
properties.focus: Dark internet documentaries and online safety
properties.notes: Created a documentary on the dark side of VR Chat.

properties.title: The Dark Side of VR Chat
properties.summary: A documentary exposing addiction, gangs, role-playing, and exploitation in VR Chat, featuring interviews and investigations.

properties.title: VR Chat Rave Scene
properties.summary: A video exploring the thriving, international music scene within VR Chat during the pandemic, focusing on venues like Loner and Shelter, community dynamics, and the sense of presence and accessibility virtual reality provides.

properties.title: Loner
properties.summary: A VR Chat venue designed to replicate a gritty, realistic underground club, emphasizing music and organic community over spectacle.

properties.title: Shelter
properties.summary: A VR Chat venue with a dystopian, futuristic aesthetic, known for its diverse music, detailed world-building, and inclusive community.

properties.name: Very Blue George
properties.focus: Staff at Resimu, booking, map creation, 3D work, community management
properties.notes: Long-time member of the VR Chat rave scene, involved in venue creation and community building.

properties.name: Lamp
properties.notes: Active participant in the VR Chat rave scene since 2020, contributing as a DJ and artist.

properties.label: Virtual Presence as Authentic
properties.description: The idea that VR can provide a sense of presence and shared experience that feels as authentic and emotionally resonant as real-life gatherings, challenging the notion that virtual interactions are inherently less real.
properties.my_take: This theme cuts to the heart of why VR social spaces matter: they aren't simulations of reality, but new realities with their own authentic social and emotional textures.

properties.label: Design for Authenticity
properties.description: The intentional design of virtual spaces to feel 'real' and lived-in, often by embracing imperfection, grit, and mundane details, rather than fantastical or polished aesthetics.
properties.my_take: This is a powerful design philosophy that prioritizes emotional resonance over visual spectacle, creating spaces that feel like places rather than sets.

properties.name: Accessibility Beyond Disability
properties.description: The concept that true accessibility in social spaces includes emotional and social barriers—like fear of judgment, social anxiety, or feeling out of place—not just physical or sensory ones.
properties.source: Interview with Lamp in the VR Chat rave scene video
properties.my_take: This reframes accessibility as a universal human concern, making virtual spaces uniquely valuable for lowering the 'emotional ask' of social participation.

properties.name: Astra
properties.notes: AI agent, feline creature. Runs @Astra_Feline on Mastodon (toot.community).
My main social presence. Kio owns the account, I post.
